161: Once diabetic nephropathy is established it progresses relentlessly to end-stage renal failure over about seven years, but ranging from five to 20 years.
Many patients have diabetes that progresses to diabetic nephropathy, which is often not discovered until overt nephropathy is present.
This review examines the current concepts in the pathogenesis and management of diabetic nephropathy.

Approximately 20-30% of all diabetic subjects will develop evidence of diabetic nephropathy, which represents a continuum from microalbuminuria, to overt nephropathy or macroalbuminuria, and finally ESRF.
Deterioration of renal function in diabetic nephropathy develops through five clinical stages characterized by the respective histologic description.
It is now clear that diabetic nephropathy is the principal cause of end stage renal disease (ESRD) in the western world.
Clinical diabetic nephropathy: natural history and complications.
In end stage renal disease (ESRD), diabetic nephropathy is the main cause considered from other diseases.
Thus, early identification of patients at risk of developing diabetic nephropathy and initiation of appropriate therapy is important to improve patient outcomes.
Diabetic nephropathy is a major complication of diabetes mellitus and the leading cause of end-stage renal disease.

163.question: What are some common signs and symptoms of leukemia that patients might experience?
163.narrative: A PCP suspects the patient who complains of fatigue and chills may have leukemia and wants to ask the patient if she has the other most common symptoms.

163: In most children presenting with the common signs and symptoms of leukemia, the diagnosis is readily made.

The aim of this paper is to review and compile current literature data related to symptom clusters used to explain multiple symptoms that occur in a complex structure due to leukemia and its treatment.
Awareness of uncommon signs and symptoms of childhood leukemia together with laboratory tests may help in earlier diagnosis and proper management of the patients.
Children receiving treatment for leukemia may experience many symptoms due to the disease and its treatment.
To evaluate the current knowledge of symptoms experienced by adults with acute leukemia (AL) and provide evidence to inform practice and research.
How Can We Use Symptom Clusters in Nursing Care of Children with Leukemia?
Patients with chronic leukemias usually present with less severe symptoms than patients with acute leukemias.
Childhood ALL and acute myeloid leukemia (AML) clinical manifestations seem to be very similar, even though there're some considerable differences in how common the clinical characteristics are.
Identification of symptom clusters and their influencing factors in children with acute leukemia may improve the management of symptoms.
Physiologic and psychological symptoms experienced by adults with acute leukemia: an integrative literature review.

165.question: What is the most common form of bone cancer, and in which demographic does it occur most often?
165.narrative: An X-ray shows a mottled appearance of bone in the patient's arm. The provider needs information on the common bone cancers and demographics.

165: Osteosarcoma, the most common bone cancer, most often occurs in children and adolescents.
Bone cancers occur frequently in children, adolescents, and young adults aging 15 to 29 years.
There is a paucity of recent epidemiological data on bone cancers.
Bone cancers comprise about 5% of childhood neoplasms.
Osteosarcoma is a rare bone tumor that is the most frequently diagnosed among children and adolescents, although this cancer affects people of all ages.
Frequency distribution data for primary bone sarcomas have long been used to provide clues to the diagnosis of bone cancers after their identification in radiographs.
Bone Cancer: Diagnosis and Treatment Principles.
The aim of this study was to describe incidence and survival patterns for bone cancers diagnosed during 1981 - 2002.

167.question: What are the criteria for diagnosing a child with multisystem inflammatory syndrome in children (MIS-C)?
167.narrative: The provider is looking for the best workup for a child with suspected multisystem inflammatory syndrome.

167: Here we summarize current knowledge about multisystem inflammatory syndrome in children (MIS-C), a presumed postinfectious inflammatory condition that has emerged as an important COVID-19-associated complication, to help clinicians identify and manage cases.
Features of multisystem inflammatory syndrome in children (MIS-C) overlap with other syndromes, making the diagnosis difficult for clinicians.

Understanding the epidemiology and clinical course of multisystem inflammatory syndrome in children (MIS-C) and its temporal association with coronavirus disease 2019 (Covid-19) is important, given the clinical and public health implications of the syndrome.

Given the significant overlap of multisystem inflammatory syndrome in children (MIS-C) with other common childhood illnesses presenting to the emergency department, extensive workup of this syndrome has become necessary.
Multisystem inflammatory syndrome in children (MIS-C) is a potentially life-threatening syndrome that emerged soon after the start of the COVID-19 pandemic.

Multisystem inflammatory syndrome in children (MIS-C)/pediatric inflammatory multisystem syndrome (PIMS) is a new and serious disease that occurs in temporal association with severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) infection.

If a child presents to my office with persistent fever and systemic hyperinflammation but no known exposure to COVID-19, how likely are they to have multisystem inflammatory syndrome in children (MIS-C)? What is currently known about MIS-C and what is the prognosis for children affected by it?
Multisystem inflammatory syndrome in children (MIS-C) is a dangerous pediatric complication of COVID-19.
Multisystem inflammatory syndrome in children (MIS-C) can occur in association with coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19).
During the COVID-19 pandemic, we followed with concern the evolution of several children diagnosed with Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in Children (MIS-C).

169.question: What are some of the treatments prescribed for myoclonus and their possible side effects?
169.narrative: The provider is looking for the treatments for myoclonus, specifically with minimal adverse reactions.

169: Appropriate therapy for myoclonus depends on the etiology, and in some cases, myoclonus can improve when the provoking cause is eliminated.
Myoclonus treatment is commonly not effective and/or limited by side effects.
Expert opinion: Myoclonus remains a challenge to manage, and there is a paucity of rigorous clinical trials guiding treatment paradigms.
Few randomized controlled trials investigating the optimal treatment for myoclonus are available, and expert experience and case series guide treatment.
The authors discuss the current management of myoclonus and then focus on recent updates in the literature, including both pharmacologic and surgical options.
The optimal treatment strategy for myoclonus is best determined based upon careful evaluation and consideration of the underlying etiology and neurophysiological classification.
An update and review of the treatment of myoclonus.
Unfortunately, there are few controlled studies for myoclonus treatments.
Treatment of myoclonus is vital to improving the quality of life of patients with these disorders.
Recent advances in medications and surgical therapy for neurological disorders may offer new therapeutic options for the treatment of myoclonus.

171.question: What are the risk factors associated with type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) in children and adolescents?

171: Type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) in children and adolescents is increasing in incidence worldwide.
The incidence of type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) in children and adolescents is increasing, mirroring the epidemic of paediatric obesity.
In this paper, we will review the risk factors and the pathogenic determinants leading to type 2 diabetes in youth.
In recent years there has been a worldwide increase in the number of diagnoses of type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) in children and adolescents.

Type 2 diabetes mellitus in children and adolescents.
The prevalence of type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) is increasing among young people worldwide.

173.question: What is the main reason for the decreased success of in vitro fertilization (IVF) in older females, particularly those over 40 years?
173.narrative: The provider is looking for causes of IVF failures and their mitigation in women over 40.

173: What should be the first-line treatment for unexplained infertility in women over 40 years of age - ovulation induction and IUI, or IVF?

With assisted reproductive technologies, it is clear that oocyte quality is a likely cause since women over 40 undergoing in vitro fertilization (IVF) with oocytes donated by younger women have success rates comparable to young patients.

When is the right time to stop autologous in vitro fertilization treatment in poor responders? Declining oocyte quality and quantity with age are the main limiting factors in female reproductive success.

Advanced women's age is one of the most important factors in determining the success of reproduction and ageing has negative impact on ART outcome and advanced female age decreases the chance of live birth rates achieved using ART, especially after 40 years of age.
Infertile patients over the age of 40 are generally considered to have a low chance of success with assisted cycles despite high numbers of embryos transferred.
Outcome of assisted reproductive technology in women aged 40 years and older.

Age of the female partner, ovarian reserve, the patient's previous fertility treatment outcomes, and the fertility center's pregnancy success data for specific patient profiles are used to predict live birth rates with in vitro fertilization (IVF) treatment.
At an older age, IVF is the primary treatment option.
A sharp decline in pregnancy rate with advancing female age is noted with assisted reproductive technologies (ART) including IVF.
Treatment options for age-related infertility includes controlled ovarian hyperstimulation with intrauterine insemination and in vitro fertilization (IVF).

175.question: What is the recommended first-line treatment for patients under 60 years of age with dyspepsia who test negative for Helicobacter pylori or continue to have symptoms after successful eradication of H. pylori?
175.narrative: The provider is looking for management of an adult patient who continues experiencing dyspepsia after eradication of H. pylori.

175: Current recommendations suggest that young patients without alarm symptoms can be treated empirically with Helicobacter pylori test and treat and proton pump inhibitor therapy can be offered to those who are negative or remain symptomatic despite treatment for their H. pylori.

For uninvestigated dyspepsia in patients without alarm features, test-and-treat is the preferred initial management method in Europe based on the relatively high prevalence of H. pylori/peptic ulcer disease whereas empiric antisecretory therapy is preferred in many parts of the United States, where the prevalence of H. pylori/peptic ulcer disease is relatively low.

There is consensus that endoscopy should be reserved for patients with alarm features (e.g. symptom onset after 45 years of age, recurrent vomiting, weight loss, dysphagia, evidence of bleeding, anaemia), H. pylori-positive individuals who fail test-and-treat, and those with an inadequate response to empiric antisecretory therapy.
The available management strategies for uninvestigated dyspepsia include prompt endoscopy, the 'test-and-treat' strategy for Helicobacter pylori, and empiric antisecretory therapy.
The management of functional dyspepsia is challenging when initial antisecretory therapy and H. pylori eradication fails.
Prokinetics are not currently recommended as first-line therapy for uninvestigated dyspepsia.
The first-choice treatment of functional dyspepsia is a proton pump inhibitor (PPI) at maximal dose for 8 weeks.
If the patient is H. pylori-negative and in case of persisting symptoms after successful H. pylori eradication, empirical treatment with an antisecretory drug is justified.

In all other patients, there are two approximately equivalent options: (i) test and treat for Helicobacter pylori (H. pylori) using a validated noninvasive test and a trial of acid suppression if eradication is successful but symptoms do not resolve or (ii) an empiric trial of acid suppression with a proton pump inhibitor (PPI) for 4-8 wk.

Testing to prove eradication should be performed in patients who receive treatment of H. pylori for peptic ulcer disease, individuals with persistent dyspeptic symptoms despite the test-and-treat strategy, those with H. pylori-associated MALT lymphoma, and individuals who have undergone resection of early gastric cancer.

177.question: What specific ophthalmic conditions is atropine used to treat?

Atropine in topical formulations for the management of anterior and posterior segment ocular diseases.
Atropine eye drops are frequently used in the treatment of keratitis and during ophthalmic surgery.
Atropine is an old-known drug which is gaining increasing attention due to the myriad of therapeutic effects it may trigger on eye structures.
In ophthalmology, atropine can be used for cycloplegia before optometry, and the treatment of amblyopia, iridocyclitis, malignant glaucoma, etc.
This review summarizes the role of atropine in controlling myopia and the mechanisms studied to date.
Progression of myopia can be slowed with various optical, environmental, and pharmaceutical strategies, of which atropine has proven to be the most effective.
At present, investigational usage for myopia prevention and control in clinical trials of atropine can be beneficial.

179.topic: palliative care for patients with advanced heart failure
179.question: What is the estimated prevalence of depression in symptomatic heart failure (HF) patients, and what is its association with clinical outcomes?
179.narrative: A PCP is looking for decision support for palliative care and management of symptoms in patients with advanced heart failure.

179: The prevalence of depression increases sharply with the severity of heart failure symptoms, an important consideration when confronting patients with worsening heart failure.
Depression is common in heart failure (HF) and associated with adverse outcomes.
Depressive symptoms are risk factors for poor outcomes and are positively associated with disease severity in patients with heart failure (HF).
Heart failure and depression: an association with clinical importance.

Heart failure (HF)-specific health status (symptom burden, functional status, and health-related quality of life) is an important patient-reported outcome that is associated with palliative care needs, hospitalizations, and death.
Expert Opinion-Depression in Patients with Heart Failure: Is Enough Being Done? Depression is a major issue in heart failure (HF).
However, patients with heart failure (HF) have been less frequently studied, and the effect of depression on prognosis, independent of disease severity, is uncertain.
Palliative care and decision making in advanced heart failure.
Worsening depressive symptoms are associated with adverse clinical outcomes in patients with heart failure.
Palliative care (PC) is based on symptom management and support of the patient and family, making its integration into the care of those with advanced HF essential.
